summary: Ronacher reflects on lessons about work, commitment, relationships, and family, aiming to offer guidance beyond engineering. He argues that fulfillment comes from deliberate commitment to meaningful work, relationships, and personal growth, not from endless optionality. Hard work is valuable when it is purposeful and reciprocated, while exploitative or meaningless work corrodes long-term well-being. He stresses that deep commitment—whether to a career path or a partner—creates depth, resilience, and trust in oneself. He concludes that a life centered on presence and commitment, rather than perfection or constant novelty, is what he wants for himself and his children.
key_points: Time invested with purpose builds mastery and enjoyment; progress matters more than perfection.
Meaningless or exploitative work destroys long-term satisfaction, regardless of short-term effort.
Deliberate commitment beats sampling: depth and mastery often outweigh hypothetical better options.
Serious relationships and family require intentional commitment, which unlocks growth and stability.
Having children reshapes priorities and demands self-care to avoid passing stress to them.
money_quotes: My core belief is that fulfillment and happiness comes from deliberate commitment to meaningful work, relationships, and personal growth and purpose.
Putting time into work and skills — and by that truly investing oneself — is always worth it.
The best way to completely destroy your long term satisfaction is if the effort you are putting into something is not reciprocated or the nature of the work feels meaningless.
Life isn't about sampling everything; it’s about making deliberate choices and committing to the ones that matter.
Value grows not with the breadth of options explored but with the depth of commitment to the path you've chosen.
That one year of intensive dating changed me.
The most important thing I learned about having children is that you first and foremost need to take care of yourself.
My life isn't about perfection or constantly chasing what's next; it's about being present and committing to the things that matter.
key_insight: Lasting fulfillment comes from intentional, reciprocated commitment to meaningful work and relationships rather than maximizing options or avoiding hard effort.
